name: Prakshit Tomar
summary: Prakshit shares how an ordinary student with a passion for volleyball transformed himself through consistent self-improvement, honesty, teamwork, and disciplined preparation to earn an NDA recommendation at AFSB Mysore.
data:
Jai Hind, ladies and gentlemen! My name is Prakshit Tomar. I am an ordinary boy from Baghpat district in western Uttar Pradesh, belonging to a middle-class family. My father is an Army veteran, and like many Fauji fathers, he was quite strict. I was never an outstanding student academically, but I loved playing volleyball and represented my school in several competitions. Although I did not score well in my Class 10 board examinations, I always dreamed of serving the nation by joining the Armed Forces.

My journey truly began when one of my seniors, who is now a Lieutenant in the Indian Army, introduced me to the various officer entry schemes. I was fascinated after learning about them, especially the National Defence Academy (NDA). That very day, I rushed home, searched everything I could about NDA, and like many aspirants, I first came across motivational edits on social media. They inspired me, but I soon realized that edits alone would never get anyone selected.

I started researching the syllabus and discovered that the mathematics and science portions were largely based on Classes 11 and 12. I also learned that clearing the written examination was only the first step and that the Service Selection Board (SSB) interview was equally important. When I asked my senior how to prepare, he recommended Pathfinder, one of the best books for defence aspirants, and advised me to start working on my personality alongside academics. I followed his advice sincerely by studying regularly, watching mock interviews, and learning from the sessions of Colonel Nehru (Retd.), a former Interviewing Officer.

Along with my studies, I continued playing volleyball and participated in several tournaments. With consistent effort, I cleared the NDA written examination while still studying in Class 12. Although I had cleared the cutoff by only a small margin, it was enough to earn my SSB call letter.

Once I qualified the written examination, I shifted my focus entirely to SSB preparation. I joined coaching, continued watching lectures by Colonel Nehru and Major General Yash Mor, and most importantly, started maintaining a daily diary. Every night, I wrote about the mistakes I had made during the day and how I could improve them. This habit gradually changed my mindset, and I began thinking more like an officer.

Initially, my father was against my decision to join the Army, but after several discussions, I convinced him to let me attend my SSB interview. Eventually, he supported me wholeheartedly and even shared valuable experiences from his own training days and his tenure in the Rashtriya Rifles. Those lessons became one of my biggest sources of motivation.

My SSB was held at AFSB Mysore, one of the most beautiful places I have ever visited. Around 120 candidates reported for the selection process. Like everyone else, I was nervous during the screening test, but somehow I managed to stay calm and cleared what many consider the toughest stage. I was allotted Chest Number 22, and the real journey began.

During the Group Testing Officer (GTO) tasks, I focused only on teamwork. My aim was never to prove myself individually but to ensure that my group completed every task successfully. I also performed well in the Lecturette and completed 11 individual obstacles, which reflected my physical fitness and confidence.

The psychology tests were next. Even today, I cannot confidently say whether my responses were exceptional or average, but I remained honest throughout. I completed all the Thematic Apperception Test (TAT) stories, attempted around 45 to 50 Situation Reaction Test (SRT) questions, and performed sincerely in the remaining psychological assessments.

The personal interview was another memorable experience. My English-speaking skills were not very strong, and I genuinely feel that improving spoken English can be extremely helpful for every defence aspirant. The Interviewing Officer asked me several tricky questions about my achievements in volleyball, my family background, financial condition, and many aspects of my personal life. One piece of advice given to me earlier by a Captain, who is now a Major, remained in my mind throughout the interview: never lie during SSB. Every answer becomes the foundation for the next question, and experienced Interviewing Officers can easily identify dishonesty. Remaining truthful not only builds credibility but also allows you to answer confidently without fear.

Conference Day was perhaps the most emotional part of my journey. Honestly, my conference did not feel particularly good. The moment I entered the room and saw all the officers looking at me, I went completely blank for a few seconds. Even today, I laugh whenever I remember that moment. When I walked out of the conference hall, I had already started thinking about which train I should book for my return journey because I believed I had not made it.

However, destiny had something else planned. Out of nearly 120 candidates, only seven were recommended, and when my chest number was announced, I simply could not believe it. It was one of the happiest moments of my life. All the hard work, discipline, failures, and self-improvement had finally paid off.

Unfortunately, my journey was not completely smooth afterward. I was declared medically unfit for the Air Force due to being overweight and having knock knees. Later, I was also merited out of the course. Despite these setbacks, receiving the recommendation itself was a life-changing achievement and taught me that determination is always rewarded.

On my journey back home, I was so happy that I bought around four to five kilograms of sweets and distributed them among fellow passengers in my train coach. That celebration remains one of my fondest memories.

If I could give one message to every defence aspirant, it would be this: work hard every single day. Watching motivational edits alone will never get you recommended. Improve yourself continuously, stay physically fit, develop your personality, be honest during the interview, work as a team, keep smiling, and never lose sight of your goal. Success belongs to those who prepare consistently and refuse to give up.

As the saying goes: 'Fire tests gold, and misfortune tests brave men.' Keep working hard, and one day, your moment of success will surely come.
